text: The laparoscopic view reveals dense peritumoral inflammatory adhesions surrounding a bulky sigmoid colon cancer, which appears non-obstructed and non-perforated. The pneumoperitoneum is maintained at a pressure between six and ten millimeters of mercury.
The laparoscopic view reveals dense peritumoral inflammatory adhesions surrounding a bulky sigmoid colon cancer, which appears non-obstructed and non-perforated.

The surgeon sets the pneumoperitoneum pressure between six and ten millimeters of mercury. Using a monopolar hook diathermy, the surgeon carefully removes inflammatory adhesions to mobilize the bulky sigmoid colon tumor laterally, taking an additional cuff of peritoneum to ensure adequate lateral margins.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook diathermy to carefully remove inflammatory adhesions, mobilizing the tumor laterally. An additional cuff of peritoneum is excised to ensure adequate lateral margins around the tumor.
The surgeon employs a lateral-to-medial approach to mobilize the bulky sigmoid colon tumor, facilitating the visualization of vessels during the procedure.
The surgeon employs a lateral-to-medial approach to mobilize the bulky sigmoid colon tumor, enhancing visualization of the surrounding vessels.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter, which has been displaced laterally by the tumor. Using a monopolar hook diatomy, the surgeon carefully dissects the tissue layer by layer to maintain the correct anatomical plane, which is distorted due to inflammatory adhesions and the tumor's weight.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter, which has been displaced laterally by the tumor. Using a monopolar hook diathermy, the surgeon carefully dissects the tissue layer by layer to maintain the correct anatomical plane, which is distorted by inflammatory adhesions and the tumor's weight.
The surgeon uses scissors to gently dissect and free a loop of small bowel that is adhered to the mass, carefully separating the bowel from the tumor.
The surgeon uses scissors to gently dissect and free a loop of small bowel that is adhered to the bulky sigmoid colon cancer mass.

Following the lateral mobilization, the surgeon turns the patient to the left, using gravity to roll the tumor away. Gentle retraction is applied to assist in removing the peritumoral inflammatory adhesions during the medial dissection.

Following lateral mobilization, the surgeon shifts focus to medial dissection. The patient is turned to the left, allowing gravity to assist in rolling the tumor away. Gentle retraction is applied to remove peritumoral inflammatory adhesions.

The surgeon identifies the tumor densely adhered to the small bowel mesentery, possibly invading it. The small bowel mesentery peritoneum is dissected en bloc with the tumor to ensure adequate resection margins.

The surgeon identifies the tumor densely adhered to the small bowel mesentery, possibly invading it. The small bowel mesentery peritoneum is dissected en bloc with the tumor to ensure adequate resection margins.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure by reflecting the greater omentum superiorly and dissecting between the greater omentum and transverse colon, entering the lesser sac. The splenic flexure is freed by resecting the spinal colic ligament.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure by reflecting the greater omentum superiorly and dissecting between the greater omentum and transverse colon, entering the lesser sac. The splenic flexure is freed by resecting the spinal colic ligament.

The surgeon dissects the tumor from its posterior retroperitoneal attachments, exposing the intact fascia over the tail of the pancreas and the left kidney. The posterior and lateral mobilization of the tumor is completed, and the posterior media dissection continues.

The surgeon dissects the tumor from its posterior retroperitoneal attachments, exposing the intact fascia over the tail of the pancreas and the left kidney. The posterior and lateral mobilization of the tumor is completed, and the posterior media dissection is continued.

The surgeon introduces a hand port through an extension of the left alec to improve retraction and facilitate the final dissection. The hand is used to enhance retraction, ensuring the tumor is adequately mobilized in all directions before this step.
The surgeon introduces a hand port through an extension of the left alec to improve retraction and facilitate the final dissection of the bulky tumor, overcoming challenges in superior retraction.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the tumor in all directions, ensuring clear access to the surgical field before introducing the hand for further retraction.

The surgeon uses palpation to safely dissect the tumor free from its remaining posterior attachments. The laparoscopic-assisted approach enhances visualization, allowing for precise mobilization of the tumor.

The surgeon uses palpation to safely dissect the tumor free from its last remaining posterior attachments. The laparoscopic-assisted approach provides enhanced visualization, allowing for precise tumor mobilization.

The surgeon locates the inferior mesenteric artery and vein within the usual anatomical plane for high ligation using a vessel sealing device. The mesocolon is divided to achieve the desired proximal margins, completing the mobilization of the tumor.

The surgeon creates a plane to locate the inferior mesenteric artery and vein, performing a high ligation using a vessel sealing device. The mesocolon is divided to achieve the desired proximal margins.
The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon cancer cranially and begins the distal total mesorectal excision (TME) using sharp dissection, continuing until an adequate distal margin is achieved.
The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon cancer cranially and begins the distal total mesorectal excision (TME) using sharp dissection, carefully achieving an adequate distal margin.

The surgical specimen is extracted through the hand port sleeve protector in the left iliac fossa. The final abdominal incisions are visible, with a local anesthetic infusion catheter placed into the main abdominal wound.

The specimen is extracted through the hand port sleeve protector located in the left iliac fossa. The final abdominal incisions are visible, along with the placement of a local anesthetic infusion catheter into the main abdominal wound.
